sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 120, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 119 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 caused the trail to extend slightly (2 cells to the right). This is different from before - previously ACTION7 didn't extend the trail. So ACTION7 seems to extend the trail by a fixed amount (2 cells?).
Looking at the image, the trail is now 2 cells long, extending right from the player.
Let me check what changed. The animation shows 12 cells changed. The trail is now visible.
So ACTION7 extends the trail by 2 cells. This is a new finding.
Now I understand: ACTION7 is like pressing RIGHT twice, but it might have special properties.
Let me try extending the trail further to touch the blocks.
Actually, wait. Looking more carefully at the image, the trail is 2 cells long. Previously when I pressed RIGHT, the trail extended 1 cell per press. So ACTION7 might be equivalent to pressing RIGHT twice.
But the key question is: what's the goal? I've been trying many approaches without success.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the goal is to have the trail touch all three blocks simultaneously? The trail is horizontal and 2 rows tall. If I position the player at a row where the trail overlaps with all three blocks... but the blocks are at different rows, so that's not possible.
Unless... the trail can be vertical? Let me try pressing ACTION7 while the player is at a different position.
Actually, let me look at the bottom panel more carefully. The blocks are shown with white outlines and connected by lines. The lines might indicate the path the trail should take. Red → Green → Blue might mean the trail should connect Red to Green to Blue.
But the trail is horizontal, so it can only connect blocks at the same row. The blocks are at different rows.
Wait, what if the trail can change direction? Let me try pressing ACTION7 to extend the trail, then pressing UP or DOWN to change the trail's direction.
Actually, I just realized something. The trail extends horizontally from the player. What if I can make the trail extend vertically by pressing UP or DOWN?
Let me try: press ACTION7 to extend the trail, then press UP or DOWN to see if the trail changes direction.
But I've been pressing UP and DOWN before, and the trail just moved vertically with the player, it didn't change direction.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. What if the goal is to push the blocks to form a horizontal line matching the bottom panel?
The bottom panel shows Red, Green, Blue from left to right. On the board, the blocks are at different rows. If I push them all to the right, they'd be at different rows but in a vertical column. To form a horizontal line, I need to move them to the same row.
But the player can only carry one block at a time when moving vertically. So I could:
1. Move to Red's row, extend RIGHT to push Red to a right position
2. Move to Green's row, extend RIGHT to push Green to a right position
3. Move to Blue's row, extend RIGHT to push Blue to a right position
4. Then move the blocks to the same row?
This is getting too complicated. Let me try something simpler.
What if the goal is to have the trail touch all three blocks, and the order doesn't matter? I've touched all three blocks already. Maybe I need to touch them all simultaneously?
Or maybe I need to touch them in a specific sequence without retracting the trail in between?
Let me try: extend the trail to touch Red, then move DOWN to touch Green (passing through Blue), then move UP to touch Blue. But I can't move vertically with the trail extended.
Wait, I just found out that I CAN'T move vertically with the trail extended (the DOWN actions were noops earlier). So the trail is locked in place horizontally once extended.
This means I can only touch blocks at the player's current row. To touch blocks at different rows, I need to retract the trail, move vertically, then extend again.
So the sequence would be:
1. Move to Red's row, extend RIGHT to touch Red
2. Retract trail, move to Green's row, extend RIGHT to touch Green
3. Retract trail, move to Blue's row, extend RIGHT to touch Blue
4. Press ACTION7 (or some other action to complete the level)
But I've tried this before and it didn't work.
Let me try pressing ACTION7 after touching all three blocks.
Actually, I just noticed something. When I pressed ACTION7, the trail extended by 2 cells. What if ACTION7 is the action that "submits" the current trail configuration? Maybe I need to have the trail touching all three blocks when I press ACTION7.
But the trail can only touch one block at a time (since blocks are at different rows).
Unless... the trail can be multi-segmented? Like, I extend the trail, retract it, extend it again, and the trail has multiple segments?
Let me check if the trail accumulates segments. Currently, the trail is 2 cells long (from the ACTION7 press). Let me extend it further and see if it accumulates.
Actually, I think the trail is just a single horizontal line from the player to the right. It doesn't accumulate segments.
OK, I'm stuck. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to use the MOUSE action to click on the blocks in the correct order?
The valid actions include MOUSE. Let me try clicking on the blocks in the order Red, Green, Blue.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# ACTION7 extended trail by 2 cells
# Let me check current state and try MOUSE on blocks

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 16 and n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try clicking on Red block first
# Red at rows 19-22, cols 18-21
# Click center: row 20, col 19
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 19}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
R: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
b: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
N: rows 37-40, cols 18-21
R: rows 57-60, cols 27-30
N: rows 57-60, cols 33-36
b: rows 57-60, cols 39-42
